original_abstract:
This sociological research survey investigates the perspectives of nurses on patient safety regarding the use of restraints in Czech hospitals. Data were collected through structured interviews and questionnaires from a representative sample of 772 general nurses working shift rotations in inpatient wards, aligned with the methodology of the Institute of Health Information and Statistics of the Czech Republic. 

The findings indicate that a majority of nurses perceive the risk of patient injury during physical restraint (specifically limb immobilization/strapping) to be either negligible (48%) or low (36.9%). However, nurses stationed in surgical and internal medicine departments reported a significantly higher likelihood of patient harm during bedside restriction compared to their colleagues in other specialties. Regarding institutional governance, 91.3% of respondents confirmed the existence of clinical standards or regulations guiding the management of restless, aggressive, or self-harming patients. Furthermore, 65.5% of nurses reported performing immobilization and restraint solely based on a physician’s order in justified cases. 

The study highlights that perceived safety risks increase in correlation with higher frequencies of unpredictable patient behavior, such as falls, aggression between patients, or suicidal tendencies. To mitigate the complications associated with restrictive practices, the authors emphasize the necessity of continuous professional education, regular nursing audits to ensure compliance with clinical standards, and the strict reservation of restraints for only the most essential and well-documented clinical circumstances.
humanized_abstract:
This sociological research survey examines the opinions of nurses on the issue of patient safety and the use of restraint measures in health care provided in Czech hospitals. The research data were collected by means of interviews and standardized questionnaires from a sample of 772 general nurses working in clinical departments in a shift regime. The composition of the sample is in line with the recommendations of the methodological procedures applied by the Institute of Health Information and Statistics of the Czech Republic.

A majority of nurses (48 %) had no fear or very low fear of that patients would be injured during physical restraint (limb immobilization/strapping) and 36.9 % had low fear that they or their colleagues would be injured while using restraint. Nurses working in surgical and internal medicine departments reported a higher probability of patient harm during the bedside restraint than nurses working in other departments. Of the participating hospitals, 91.3 % confirmed that some kind of clinical guidelines or rules for the care of restless, aggressive or self-abusive patients existed in their department. Fifty-five per cent of nurses restrained/immobilized patients (65.5 % only on doctor’s orders) on a weekly or more frequent basis.

Risk assessments for safety are higher when patients exhibit unusual behaviour more frequently – such as falling, aggressive behaviour between patients or suicidal behaviour. The authors suggest that in order to minimise the negative consequences of restricting patients’ liberty, healthcare staff should undertake regular training, participate in audits to ensure they are working within established clinical guidelines and that the use of restraint is limited to situations where these are strictly necessary and duly recorded.
